glossary.def: The probability distribution over a parameter before observing data, encoding what you believe (or assume) at the outset. With little data it dominates the posterior; with enough data any non-dogmatic prior washes out.
The probability a model assigns to the observed data as a function of the parameter, p(D | θ). It is the channel through which evidence pulls the prior toward whatever the data support.
The updated belief about a parameter after seeing data, p(θ | D) ∝ p(D | θ) p(θ). Unlike a point estimate it is a full distribution, so uncertainty is reported directly.

A prior whose family is preserved under the likelihood, so the posterior stays in the same family and updating is pure arithmetic on the parameters. Beta–Binomial and Normal–Normal are the canonical pairs.
Maximum a posteriori: the mode of the posterior, equal to the MLE with the log-prior added back in. It coincides with the MLE under a flat prior and is the Bayesian reading of regularized estimation.

An interval that contains the parameter with a stated posterior probability given the data, e.g. P(θ ∈ [L,U] | D) = 0.95 — the direct probability statement a confidence interval is often mistaken for.
A frequentist interval whose stated level describes the long-run coverage of the procedure across repeated experiments, not the probability that this particular interval contains the fixed parameter.

A multi-level model in which group parameters are drawn from a shared hyper-prior, letting estimates borrow strength across groups (partial pooling) and shrink noisy small samples toward the population mean.

refs.cite: Bayes, T. & Price, R. (1763). An Essay towards solving a Problem in the Doctrine of Chances. Phil. Trans. R. Soc.
Gelman, A., Carlin, J. B., Stern, H. S., Dunson, D. B., Vehtari, A. & Rubin, D. B. (2013). Bayesian Data Analysis (3rd ed.). CRC Press.
Jaynes, E. T. (2003). Probability Theory: The Logic of Science. Cambridge University Press.
Efron, B. & Morris, C. (1975). Data Analysis Using Stein's Estimator and Its Generalizations. JASA.
Casella, G. & Berger, R. L. (1987). Reconciling Bayesian and Frequentist Evidence in the One-Sided Testing Problem. JASA.
Carpenter, B. et al. (2017). Stan: A Probabilistic Programming Language. J. Stat. Softw.
